sentences.text: This one barn-- like I was telling you about-- this one man, it's-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] of all-- it's-- it's-- I don't know.
It's hard to say-- you ignored the rest of them.
But this one was the one that got my attention.
And-- You would think that they would have been overjoyed to see us.
But then you wonder, did they even know who we were?
Were their minds gone?
Were their other human capabilities-- they were so-- so-- so close to death that you wonder sometimes what they were even thinking.
And I know other places, they say when they came in, they were overjoyed, and-- and ran around.

But when you see something where people don't do much-- of course, in this camp it was a little bit different, because the only ones left there supposedly were dead or had been-- had been shot just before the-- the Germans pulled out.
So maybe they were too far gone to be-- to be happy.
But you always wonder, did any of them make it?
You know, I sure hope so.
God.
Do you-- do you recall what the date was when you entered the camp?
Not the exact date.
It was about the-- the 1st-- almost the-- about the 1st of April.
I-- I know-- if it was the first, second, third, something-- I don't know.
Eisenhower didn't go in until-- [SIGHS] I think the 11th or 12th.
What happened [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the reports-- of course, when-- after we-- you know, we were working.
I mean, we weren't infantry.
We had a job to do.
We-- we had to get this telephone line in.
I think most of the other guys there were-- could have been infantry, or more combat type-- not that we weren't combat.
But we-- we weren't in this-- the handgun shooting type, the front lines.
We were back a little bit.
So actually, I think after we left, they brought the medics and stuff in.
And of course, then headquarters found out.
And then Patton went.
One-- I don't know if he went that first day, or he went the next day, or what it was.
But then he tried to get Eisenhower to come.
And Eisenhower didn't want to.
And I guess it took a little bit of coaxing.
And finally, Eisenhower said, OK, I'll come.
If it's what you say it is, the only reason I'm-- now I'm telling you this from what I've read.
Eisenhower said that he was just going to do it, so he personally could vouch for what-- what was being seen.
And so then that's when he went in.
Eisenhower, Patton, Bradley-- I don't know.
One of-- one of my buddies was there, at that time when Eisenhower was there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I've looked for his picture in-- in, you know, the newsreels and stuff.
I've got quite a collection of all-- of tapes I've been collecting over the years.
But I've never seen him.
But there were so many at that time.
Of course, when I took my picture, there was very few-- I don't think there's a GI on my picture at all.
I don't think more than 20-- 25 at the most.
I think it was a nice day.
It was cold.
No, I don't.
I don't know.
A mile or so, maybe?
I don't know.
It's-- if I knew then what I know now, I would have taken more details.
I would wrote stuff down.
But it's-- we didn't know what we were getting into.
We didn't know we were making history.
[PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] So you know, hindsight's a lot better than foresight then-- so a lot-- lot of little details you don't-- you don't remember.
I can remember going back to Rome.
I knew where all the places were.
But I didn't know how to get there.
I'd been there.
But even when I went back to Vienna, I went back to the house that I lived in-- well, it's not a house.
It's like an apartment.
But they don't have houses like we do in town.
And I had to stand there.
And I-- I just closed my eyes and-- and tried to go back 40 some years, and just to picture what-- what I did when I came in the building.
Because your memory sometimes-- little-- little things like that you don't pay no attention to, that you do every day.
So a lot of things about the camps and stuff-- I do remember that there were buildings along-- looked like a solid wall of buildings.
There was no fence, per se.
It was like the backs of buildings, with this big gate.
Like I said, I don't remember any sign over the top, which they say every camp in Germany had this-- the sign, 'work will make you free,' [GERMAN].
I don't remember that.
But once inside the camp, these-- most of this stuff is-- it just burnt right into my brain.
I think it was the shock-- the shock effect.
I mean, the shock maybe burnt it in, where some of the details outside and stuff-- what happened before, what happened after-- are so immaterial that I just-- you just don't pay no attention to it.
I mean, we lived day by day.
And we were lucky to see another morning.
And so a lot of the details I-- I don't-- I don't really remember.
But the details, the pictures inside-- I could sit down and draw you pictures.
It's-- they're that-- almost that clear in my mind yet.
Mm mm.
We didn't personally.
Like I said, there weren't very many walking around.
There was only just a very, very few-- if there was a half a dozen walking around, that was a lot.
And they were in no condition.

I-- I'm sure some of them-- when more people probably got in there that could-- could speak German or something, it may have-- may have made a difference the-- to find out-- I don't-- we don't know what nationalities they were, whether they were Jewish, or political, or-- or what.
We don-- we-- there-- we don't know too much about Ohrdruf.
That's the only camp that they don't have the statistics-- how many people were-- had gone through the camp, who the people were.
Most of the other camps, they have all the records.
They can tell you who went through the camp.
Because they took excellent records.
And-- but I don't know why, that being the first camp, they took everything, and--
Oh, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] on the-- more or less the barracks type of buildings, there must have been [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a couple dozen, at least.
It-- it was pretty good sized camp.
And these big barn-type buildings-- I-- I-- I don't know how many there were.
Like I say, most of them had-- had bunks, but this-- this one-- one or two didn't have any bunks in them.
It was just-- just straw, more or less.
We weren't looking at the buildings.
[LAUGHS]
I didn't go in.
But some of the guys did go in.
And I heard that they had found some lampshades made out of tattoos and stuff.
But I-- I didn't see any of those myself.
But they said there were some.
I-- I think most of these inmates-- they worked in a factory nearby.
It was a-- it was slave labor type thing.
About two or three hours.
We-- then we-- then we had a-- we couldn't spend any more time.
We had to get those wires laid.
Because we were moving so fast in-- so we left.
There were other units there.
There-- actually, I don't know how many guys from different units were there.
It was one of those things that we just happened to-- everybody just kept rolling in and rolling in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, you-- it spread pretty fast.
And I-- I don't remember much after that.
And I don't remember what-- really much what happened after that, in our unit type thing.
Oh, yeah.
Everybody was talking about it.
No-- nobody could believe it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I still-- as-- now, we weren't up high.
We did have a one-star general that commanded the artillery in our battery.
But we had-- we didn't have any access to any big rumors, like were going around, and stuff like that.
So I-- I would say it took quite a-- not days, or months, or anything like that.
But it took quite a while for-- to sink in, what was really go on.

Even when we-- you got back to so-called higher echelons in our own division, I don't realize-- I don't think they realize, until it got to maybe Third Army, Patton himself, that they knew what was going on, really.
They found something.
But I don't think they knew what they found.
Almost disbelief.
And-- and then afterwards, some-- some of our group did go back, and go in.
Of course, afterwards, once Eisenhower got through-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one source says, Eisenhower said it.
Another source says, Patton said it.
But it doesn't make any difference who said it.
But they gave the order that everybody possible was supposed to go through.
And they-- they start bringing the civilians in from town, and GIs-- American, British, French, whoever.
Anyone in the vicinity came in.
So some of our other guys did-- most of the guys I've talked to at our reunions-- very few of them actually went through.
The ones that did were afterwards.
It-- we just happened to-- our wire crew just happened to be in the right place, I guess, in the right time.
I don't know.
If-- if you-- you can call that the right-- you know, being right.
Where did you-- where did the unit go from there?
We-- we paused, just kind of regroup, after pulling back.
And we-- we turned right around.
Instead of going almost due north to Berlin, we went right, straight down the middle of Germany, through Bamberg, Nuremberg, Munich area.
Although we didn't go right into Munich.
We took Regensburg.
And the-- the day the war ended, we had just-- just taken Linz, Austria.
So all in all, the 65th made through Germany, traveled 650 some odd miles.
That's more than any other unit.
Because we-- we went from the German border, almost-- well, practically almost spit on Berlin, practically, and then came right, straight down the middle.
And by the time we stopped, we were trying to meet the Russians.
And we had advance groups almost in Vienna.
So that's a long, long way.
I didn't personally.
But the 65th has-- we didn't get any credit for Ohrdruf.
Although, we did have people there.
We got credit for two-- two other camps, similar to Ohrdruf, that were slave labor camps.
We were in on Mauthausen, in northern Austria.
That was where the big stone quarry was.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
So there was three or four.
There were a couple that we were there, but didn't get cre-- maybe we didn't have enough guys there.
Maybe we didn't have enough guys at Ohrdruf.
But most of our-- or I think most of our records were lost in the fire in St.
Louis.
So it's kind of-- so we-- we had to rely mostly on our own records to get-- to get the recognition.
Our flag is-- is now in the-- the Holocaust Museum, in Washington.
It's one of the ones that flies there.
Did you, yourself, encounter any other camps?
No, that was the only one.
We were mad as hell, I'll tell you.
You know, it's-- it's a-- especially if you had Jewish guys in your outfit.
And we had maybe half a dozen.
It-- it was-- it was hard.
You talk to the German people.
And they could live right outside the gate.
And they swore up and down they didn't know anything about it.
Maybe some of them didn't.
But you-- you-- you can't smell burning flesh and not know something's going on, or see a-- a railroad train come in, day, after day, after day, and bringing people in, and don't bring anybody out.
There is such thing as not wanting to know.
Because if they knew, they might do something.
And they-- they could end up in the place, themselves.
Because the German-- Hitler threw just as many Germans, almost, as anybody else in-- his own people.
It wasn't-- it wasn't just Jews, and-- and Polish, and Czechs, and Slavs, and gypsies, and stuff like that.
A lot of them were-- were his own people.
Yeah, mostly after the war, after, you know-- after combat was over.
And then we had German-- I lived in a little town in northern Austria, just, oh, within walking distance of where Hitler was born.
And we-- we didn't have any KP, or anything.
The German civilians did all our work for us.
We-- and in turn, they-- they got food and stuff, too.
Of course, a lot of guys were shacking up with the girls and stuff and you know.
But I was too young.
I was only-- I was fresh out of the convent school.
[LAUGHS] But that's beside the point.
But we-- we had a lot of DPs around that-- that first summer.
They were--
Displaced persons--
--that had come in.
Most of them were Hungarian.
We had a lot of kids.
We had a swimming pool and we let the-- not too many adults came to swim in it, But a lot of kids.
I got pictures-- a lot of the-- the little kids-- seven, eight years old, you know-- around that area.
Not that-- not that I know.
I didn't run into any.
I don't-- I don't know where these people came from.
They just came out of the-- the cracks in the wall, it seemed like.
They just-- they just appeared.
The place we were was a sulfur bath resort af-- we-- the first summer.
And they just came out of the-- out of the woodwork.
Of course, they-- they had to go someplace where people would give them food.
Because there was-- there was nothing doing.
There was no work.
The war ended.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We had just taken Linz.
And the 65th division was practically the whole north end of Austria.
We-- that whole area was our-- our front.
And of course, when the war ended, we just stayed put for a week or so.
I personally was in the monastery at St.
Florian.
That's where Anton Bruckner, the famous Austrian composer, was the organist.
I got to play his organ up at the church one day.
A couple nuns took me up and showed me it and asked me if I wanted to play it.
And I says, sure.
And-- and then we went a-- another town, north-- kind of Northwest of Linz, Bad Schallerbach.
It was a sulfur bath resort.
We stayed there for the summer.
And then we moved around again.
And then we were packing to go to Japan.
And about I know-- end of July, first of August, in that area somewhere-- we got orders, that's it.
Your division's being deactivated.
That's it.
We're not going to Japan.
And so most of the guys were all old enough to be my father, practically.
They all went home and-- and about three of us kids stayed in one room for about a week.
And then another division moved in.
Then they went home.
We sat in the-- for another week, by ourselves.
And another division moves in.
And then finally, they sent me to Vienna.
I was General Clark's chief clerk, in his private office.
My desk was right outside of his-- his office door.
Sergeant.
Six months.
I came home and got married.
[LAUGHS]
